title: Meringue kisses
subtitle: Small piped meringue shells, crisp throughout
excerpt: Meringue kisses are small, crisp meringues piped into rosettes or teardrops and baked until dry all the way through. They are gluten-free, last for a week in a tin, and make excellent gifts and decorations. Colour them with a stripe of gel food colouring painted inside the piping bag.
sourceNotes: Small piped meringue kisses are a British baking tradition adapted from the French pâtisserie technique of using a star tip to pipe meringue into small rosettes. The method is consistent across twentieth-century British baking books. The coloured stripe technique using food colouring painted inside the bag became popular in the 2010s. This recipe follows the established British small-meringue technique.

recipe.batchNotes: Double the batch to fill two trays; the bake time is the same.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Store in an airtight container at room temperature for up to 1 week.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven only; fan will dry the outside too quickly and cause cracking
baking.decoratingTechnique: piped rosettes with optional gel colour stripe

glossaryTerms.definition: Egg whites whipped to stiff peaks will hold a firm, upright point when the whisk is lifted. Unlike soft peaks, which fold over, stiff peaks stay rigid. For small meringues that need to hold a piped shape, the whites must reach stiff peaks; undermixed whites will cause the piped shape to spread and lose definition. The meringue is ready when you can hold the bowl upside down without it moving.

content.text: Meringue kisses need the whites taken to
to hold their shape through piping and baking. The ratio here is the classic two-to-one of sugar to egg whites by weight; anything less and the kisses will not be crisp throughout.

For the coloured stripe effect, dip a clean pastry brush in gel food colouring and paint two or three lines from the tip to the base on the inside of the piping bag before filling. When the meringue is piped, the colour stripes run up the outside of each rosette.

Heat the oven to 100 degrees C (conventional). Line two baking trays with baking paper. Wipe the bowl and whisk with a cloth dipped in a little cider vinegar to remove any grease. Whisk the 2 egg whites and quarter teaspoon cream of tartar on medium speed until the mixture forms soft peaks. Switch to high speed and add the 110 g caster sugar one tablespoon at a time, whisking well after each addition. When all the sugar is in, add the vanilla extract if using and continue whisking for 2 more minutes until the meringue is very thick, glossy, and holds stiff, upright peaks.

Fit a piping bag with a star nozzle. Twist the bag closed at the top and pipe small rosettes about 3 cm wide onto the trays, spacing them a centimetre apart. They will not spread during baking. Bake for 1 hour 30 minutes until dry and completely crisp throughout. Turn off the oven and leave inside with the door ajar for 30 minutes before removing. Store in an airtight container once completely cool.

items.problem: Meringues weep or have sticky pools on the surface
items.solution: The sugar was not fully dissolved. Rub a pinch of meringue between two fingers; it should feel completely smooth. Add the sugar more slowly on the next batch, whisking for at least 15 seconds after each spoonful before adding the next.
The whites were not whipped to stiff peaks. The mixture should be so thick it sits in a peak without drooping. If the meringue slides off the whisk it is not ready.
